findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether compute_plan_review_validity is a rename of the existing compute_validity (and enumerate the affected call-sites including completion.py:176), a new wrapper delegating to it, or a parallel function — and update the plan to explicitly address the migration of the existing boundary.
evidence: Zero matches for 'compute_plan_review_validity' in src/ — the name does not exist
compute_validity exists at src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py:471 with signature (attestation, ticket_state, kind, *, repo_root=None)
src/rebar/llm/completion.py:176 calls compute_validity with kind='completion-verifier' — an external caller that would be affected by any rename/split
10+ test files import compute_validity from rebar.llm.plan_review.attest directly
findings.finding: The plan states 'Own one compute_plan_review_validity profile boundary used by claim, reuse, drift refresh, re-sign, audit/health, and close' as though compute_plan_review_validity is the existing boundary, but the actual codebase boundary is compute_validity (attest.py:471) with 10+ external call-sites including rebar/llm/completion.py:176 (outside plan_review/) using kind='completion-verifier'. The plan never names compute_validity, never states it will be renamed/wrapped/replaced, and never addresses the completion-verifier call-site that uses the same function. This makes the relationship between the proposed compute_plan_review_validity and the existing compute_validity unspecified.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Own one compute_plan_review_validity profile boundary used by claim, reuse, drift refresh, re-sign, audit/health, and close'
scenarios: If the implementor renames compute_validity to compute_plan_review_validity, the completion.py:176 call-site and all test imports break silently unless also updated — work the plan never mentions.
If the implementor adds a new compute_plan_review_validity alongside the existing compute_validity, the 'one boundary' goal is not achieved and the two functions may diverge.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether `compute_plan_review_validity` replaces or wraps the existing `compute_validity` in `attest.py`, and enumerate the callers that must be updated (`completion.py`, `claim_gate_check`, `_attested_delivered`, `drift_refresh_candidate`).
evidence: No grep hit for `compute_plan_review_validity` anywhere in src/
Existing function: `compute_validity` at attest.py:471
Current callers of compute_validity: completion.py:174, attest.py:653,759, transition_close.py (comment at 543)
findings.finding: The plan names `compute_plan_review_validity` as the unified boundary, but this symbol does not exist — the codebase has `compute_validity` (in `attest.py`) which already serves plan-review and completion-verifier. The plan does not specify whether `compute_plan_review_validity` replaces, wraps, or is renamed from `compute_validity`, leaving the migration path for all existing callers (including `completion.py`, `_attested_delivered`, `drift_refresh_candidate`, `claim_gate_check` in `attest.py`) ambiguous.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Own one compute_plan_review_validity profile boundary used by claim, reuse, drift refresh, re-sign, audit/health, and close.'
scenarios: If compute_plan_review_validity is a rename of compute_validity, existing completion-verifier callers would need to be updated or the function needs to keep dispatching both kinds.
If it is a new wrapper, the plan needs to enumerate which callers switch over and which stay on compute_validity.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `drift_refresh_candidate` (or `drift_refresh`) gates on `compute_plan_review_validity` pin health so that enabled pin drift blocks the progressive drift-refresh path, not just claim/reuse/re-sign.
evidence: Plan: 'Own one compute_plan_review_validity profile boundary used by claim, reuse, drift refresh, re-sign, audit/health, and close.'
drift_refresh_candidate (attest.py:146-179) checks only `manifest_regver` and `manifest_material` — no pin check exists or is mentioned as being added.
AC: 'Enabled pin drift prevents progressive drift refresh.' — no mechanism is given for how drift_refresh_candidate or drift_refresh enforces this.
drift_refresh (orchestrator.py:581-675) calls drift_refresh_candidate, then runs a probe — there is no validity pre-gate calling compute_plan_review_validity.
findings.finding: The mechanism for AC 'Enabled pin drift prevents progressive drift refresh' is unspecified: `drift_refresh_candidate` (attest.py:146-179) currently returns a non-None candidate based only on material+regver checks — it never checks pin health. Under the new plan, if enforcement is enabled and pins have drifted, `drift_refresh_candidate` would still return a valid candidate and `drift_refresh` would proceed to the probe, violating the AC. The plan does not say whether `drift_refresh_candidate` itself should call `compute_plan_review_validity` before returning, whether `drift_refresh` should call it as a pre-gate, or whether the profile check is threaded through the candidate return value.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'drift refresh' consumer / AC: 'Enabled pin drift prevents progressive drift refresh'
scenarios: Enforcement is enabled; a prerequisite target's material drifted (stale-pin-drift); drift_refresh_candidate returns a non-None candidate (material+regver still match); drift_refresh runs a probe and refreshes the attestation without ever blocking on pin drift — AC violated.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence: 'Before returning a refreshable candidate, `drift_refresh_candidate` calls `compute_plan_review_validity` with the drift-refresh profile (enforcement check enabled); a non-current pin_status causes it to return None (→ full review), consistent with how material/regver mismatches already block the candidate.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify why `compute_plan_review_validity` must be a new function rather than an extended `compute_validity(…, profile=…)`, and specify the migration or aliasing plan for all existing call-sites (claim, reuse, drift-refresh, re-sign, close, audit, completion-verifier).
evidence: compute_validity exists at src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py:471 with 6+ known call-sites
Plan names 'compute_plan_review_validity' as if it is a new function, distinct from compute_validity, with no retirement or alias strategy stated
No codebase search match for 'compute_plan_review_validity'
findings.finding: The plan introduces `compute_plan_review_validity` as a new named boundary but the codebase already has `compute_validity` at `attest.py:471`, called by `claim_gate_check`, `_idempotent_reuse`, `drift_floor_candidate`, `_attested_delivered`, and the completion path. The plan does not explain why `compute_validity` cannot be extended with a profile/mode parameter instead of being replaced or aliased, nor does it state the migration path for all existing call-sites. This is a novel-architecture decision with no prior-art justification vs the obvious alternative (extend the existing function).
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Own one compute_plan_review_validity profile boundary used by claim, reuse, drift refresh, re-sign, audit/health, and close.'
scenarios: Implementer creates a second validity function alongside compute_validity, leading to divergence and duplicate logic across the six consumers
Existing callers (completion_sidecar, _attested_delivered, drift_floor_candidate) silently continue calling compute_validity instead of the new boundary, undermining the 'single boundary' goal

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a precedence/aggregation rule specifying how per-TargetPinDetail `pin_status` values roll up to the top-level `DerivedPlanReviewHealth.pin_status` (e.g., first-error-wins in defined priority order: malformed-pin > stale-pin-missing > stale-pin-drift > current).
evidence: Plan text: 'pin_status exactly current, stale-pin-drift, stale-pin-missing, malformed-pin, or legacy-unpinned'

Plan text: 'TargetPinDetail is exactly {canonical_id: string, role: child|prerequisite, pinned_fingerprint: string, current_fingerprint: string|null, pin_status: current|stale-pin-drift|stale-pin-missing|malformed-pin}'
Plan text: 'legacy-unpinned has an empty targets list'
ABSENCE: No precedence rule is stated for mixed-status targets lists (e.g., first-error-wins, worst-status-wins, or ordered priority).
This is a processing protocol that belongs co-located with the DerivedPlanReviewHealth schema definition but is entirely absent.
findings.finding: The plan defines `DerivedPlanReviewHealth.pin_status` with five values (current, stale-pin-drift, stale-pin-missing, malformed-pin, legacy-unpinned) but provides no aggregation rule specifying how per-target `TargetPinDetail.pin_status` values roll up into the top-level `pin_status` field. For example: when one target is `stale-pin-drift` and another is `malformed-pin`, which top-level status wins? The rule 'legacy-unpinned has an empty targets list' only covers that one variant.
findings.location: Implementation section — DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract definition
scenarios: An attestation with both a stale-drifted child pin and a malformed prerequisite pin: implementers will diverge on which top-level pin_status to return.
A renderer inspects `pin_status` at the top level expecting a deterministic value; two implementations disagree, breaking the 'deterministic' AC.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract definition: 'Top-level pin_status is the highest-severity status across all targets in priority order: malformed-pin > stale-pin-missing > stale-pin-drift > current; legacy-unpinned is only possible when the targets list is empty.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State explicitly whether `compute_plan_review_validity` renames, wraps, or replaces `compute_validity`, and specify how the existing `completion-verifier` kind path and external callers (e.g., completion.py) are handled.
evidence: Plan text: 'Own one compute_plan_review_validity profile boundary used by claim, reuse, drift refresh, re-sign, audit/health, and close.'
src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py:471: `def compute_validity(` — confirmed real function with 6+ call-sites in the same file plus external callers.
src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py:653: `compute_validity(sig, ticket, 'completion-verifier', ...)` — completion-verifier path in the same function body.
ABSENCE: No plan statement about whether compute_validity is retired, renamed, or preserved alongside the new boundary.
findings.finding: The plan introduces `compute_plan_review_validity` as a new named profile boundary but never specifies its relationship to the existing `compute_validity` function confirmed in attest.py:471 (which has 6+ confirmed call-sites including completion.py:176 outside plan_review/). No disposition is stated: is `compute_plan_review_validity` a rename, a wrapper, or a replacement? An implementer reading both the plan and the code cannot determine how to migrate existing call-sites.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Own one compute_plan_review_validity profile boundary' paragraph
scenarios: An implementer creates a new compute_plan_review_validity alongside compute_validity; callers diverge between the two, defeating the 'single boundary' goal.

The completion-verifier kind handling inside compute_validity must NOT receive pin checks (the plan excludes it); a naive rename/wrapper would need to branch on kind — but that branch rule is never stated.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a sentence to the Implementation section describing what 'no warning event is persisted for derived health' means (which event/log system, what is suppressed, and why), so the AC is grounded in described work.
evidence: AC text: 'No warning event is persisted for derived health.'

Implementation section describes the DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract shape and enforcement modes but contains no description of event-persistence behavior, logging, or suppression for the derived health path.
ABSENCE: No Implementation sentence addresses event persistence for derived health.
findings.finding: The AC 'No warning event is persisted for derived health' has no corresponding described deliverable in the Implementation section; no part of the Implementation describes event-persistence behavior for derived health, making this an orphan criterion.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: 'No warning event is persisted for derived health'
scenarios: Implementer has no guidance on what 'persisted' means (database row, audit log entry, event bus message) or what the correct suppression mechanism is.
The criterion cannot be verified without knowing what system it targets.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete mechanism by which the close profile suppresses code-freshness checks (e.g., a new `check_code_freshness` keyword on the renamed boundary function, a profile enum value, or a separate close-profile function), since no such parameter or pattern currently exists in compute_validity or transition_close.py.
evidence: Zero matches for 'check_code_freshness' across all of src/
compute_validity signature at attest.py:471: def compute_validity(attestation, ticket_state, kind, *, repo_root=None) — no check_code_freshness parameter
transition_close.py:543 calls compute_validity without any such flag
Plan states 'close profile sets check_code_freshness=false' as a present-tense configuration of an existing interface
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'the close profile sets check_code_freshness=false while retaining signature, registry, own-material, and configured pin checks' but no parameter or attribute named check_code_freshness exists anywhere in the codebase (zero grep matches in src/). The close consumer in transition_close.py calls compute_validity with no such parameter, and compute_validity's signature at attest.py:471 has no such keyword argument. This is an unbacked implementation assertion about a specific named interface element that must be invented from scratch, yet is stated as if it is an existing knob to toggle.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'The close profile sets check_code_freshness=false while retaining signature, registry, own-material, and configured pin checks'
scenarios: An implementor reading the plan will look for the existing check_code_freshness hook in compute_validity and find nothing, requiring them to invent a new parameter name — or the plan intends a different mechanism entirely (e.g., a profile enum or a separate function overload) that is unspecified.

The close-consumer AC ('Enabled pin drift invalidates the close-profile consumer when that gate is active') depends on the close profile correctly suppressing code-freshness checks, but the mechanism for doing so is unverified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the specific file(s) that constitute the 'audit/health' consumer (e.g. `src/rebar/audit/page.py`, `src/rebar/_cli/_audit_commands.py`, or a new module) so they can be confirmed as edit targets.
evidence: No grep hit for plan_review_health or health in plan_review/
src/rebar/audit/page.py exists but has no pin-health surface
test_audit_plan_review_v2.py exists but covers sidecar v2, not pin health
AC: 'Derived health reports enabled pin drift as invalid' and 'Audit reports enabled pin drift as invalid' suggest two separate surfaces
findings.finding: The plan names `audit/health` as one of the six consumers of `compute_plan_review_validity`, but no existing audit-health endpoint or function for plan-review pin health can be found in the codebase. If this is a new surface, the file targets for it (e.g. `src/rebar/audit/page.py`, audit CLI, or a new module) are not enumerated.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Own one compute_plan_review_validity profile boundary used by … audit/health'
scenarios: If 'audit/health' is a new endpoint requiring changes to audit/page.py or a new audit function, those files should be in the edit targets.

If it refers to the DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract returned alongside compute_plan_review_validity (per 'Own the nested DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract returned as health beside compute_plan_review_validity'), then 'audit' as a consumer is ambiguous — is it a separate audit CLI command, the existing test_audit_plan_review_v2 path, or something else?

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the fail-open vs. fail-closed ambiguity for the case where enforce_plan_material_pins was successfully enabled but config is transiently unreadable at validation time — specify which policy wins and add an AC covering this scenario.
evidence: 'Keep optional unreadable configuration fail-open with a warning, matching existing gate posture; once enforcement is successfully enabled, validation fails closed.'
ABSENCE: no AC or implementation text resolves the transient-unreadable case after enforcement has been set to true — is it fail-open or fail-closed?
findings.finding: The plan states two conflicting policies for unreadable configuration: 'keep optional unreadable configuration fail-open with a warning' (general rule) and 'once enforcement is successfully enabled, validation fails closed' (post-enable rule). The behavior when enforcement has been previously enabled but config becomes transiently unreadable at validation time is unspecified — a consumer could silently bypass enforcement via a transient config-read failure, defeating the closed-failure guarantee.
findings.location: Implementation section, paragraph: 'Keep optional unreadable configuration fail-open with a warning, matching existing gate posture; once enforcement is successfully enabled, validation fails closed.'
scenarios: Config store becomes transiently unavailable after enforcement is enabled; validation silently reverts to fail-open, allowing drifted attestations to pass.
Adversarial or accidental config corruption after enforcement is enabled causes all validation to bypass pin checks.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a spec sentence: 'If enforcement was last successfully read as enabled and config subsequently becomes unreadable, validation MUST fail closed (not open); fail-open applies only when enforcement has never been successfully set.' Add a corresponding AC: 'Config unreadable after enforcement was enabled: validation fails closed.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the fallback decision rule when a pinned target's current fingerprint cannot be computed due to a read error (distinct from 'target is deleted'): state whether this maps to `stale-pin-missing`, a new status, or a fail-open advisory.
evidence: Plan text: 'current_fingerprint: string|null' — null is allowed but the plan only explains the null case as stale-pin-missing (deleted/missing pinned target).
Plan text: 'Archived targets remain readable.'
Plan text: 'Keep optional unreadable configuration fail-open with a warning' — this covers config, not target-fingerprint reads.
ABSENCE: No specification of what pin_status or what current_fingerprint value to use when a target exists but its fingerprint computation fails (I/O error, malformed state).
findings.finding: The plan specifies that `current_fingerprint` in `TargetPinDetail` is `string|null` but never specifies the fallback decision rule when a pinned target's fingerprint CANNOT BE READ at health-compute time (e.g., due to a read error, not because the target is deleted). The plan states 'archived targets remain readable' and the config fail-open rule, but neither covers a transient read failure on a live related target.
findings.location: Implementation section — TargetPinDetail schema and 'When enabled, drift ... invalidate' paragraph
scenarios: A read error on a pinned child's state at health-compute time: should current_fingerprint be null (treated as stale-pin-missing, invalidating under enforcement) or should the sub-step fail open?

The 'Malformed manifest metadata returns malformed/invalid at every consumer without raising' AC implies no exceptions — but a read error on the target itself is a separate sub-step with no specified fallback.

findings.finding: The `current_fingerprint: string|null` field in TargetPinDetail is defined as null-capable, but the plan gives no decision rule distinguishing the null cases: I/O error on fingerprint recompute vs. target deleted vs. target archived. The enforcement branch ('deleted/missing pinned target invalidate') requires distinguishing deleted from archived, but both would present as null or unreadable without a disambiguation rule.
findings.location: Implementation — TargetPinDetail definition: 'current_fingerprint: string|null'; also 'Archived targets remain readable'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expand the per-consumer invalidation ACs (or add dedicated ACs) to cover stale-pin-missing and malformed-pin states, matching the three-state invalidation contract described in the Implementation.
evidence: AC set uses 'pin drift' uniformly: 'Enabled pin drift invalidates the claim consumer', 'Enabled pin drift invalidates the close-profile consumer', etc.
Implementation: 'When enabled, drift, malformed pins, or a deleted/missing pinned target invalidate the attestation.'

The AC 'Missing/deleted, archived, malformed, duplicate, and re-enable scenarios are deterministic' partially covers these but does not explicitly assign invalidation per consumer for missing/malformed states.
findings.finding: The six consumer-invalidation ACs (claim, close, reuse, drift-refresh, re-sign, audit, derived health) all specify only 'pin drift' (stale-pin-drift), but the Implementation describes three invalidating states: drift, deleted/missing (stale-pin-missing), and malformed pins — the ACs do not cover the missing and malformed states for each consumer.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: 'Enabled pin drift invalidates the claim consumer' (and five analogous ACs); Implementation: 'When enabled, drift, malformed pins, or a deleted/missing pinned target invalidate the attestation'
scenarios: An implementation that only invalidates on stale-pin-drift but silently passes stale-pin-missing or malformed-pin would satisfy all six consumer ACs while violating the Implementation intent.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Split the bundled AC into per-scenario ACs with observable outcomes, e.g.: 'Missing pinned target with enforcement enabled returns pin_status=stale-pin-missing and valid=false'; 'Archived pinned target returns pin_status=current and valid=true'; etc.
evidence: 'Missing/deleted, archived, malformed, duplicate, and re-enable scenarios are deterministic' — five discrete behavioral scenarios in one AC
'deterministic' does not name an observable system state (e.g., which pin_status value, which valid/invalid result)
findings.finding: AC 'Missing/deleted, archived, malformed, duplicate, and re-enable scenarios are deterministic' bundles at least five distinct scenarios into a single checkbox and uses the subjective/vague term 'deterministic' rather than stating the specific observable outcome for each scenario.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Missing/deleted, archived, malformed, duplicate, and re-enable scenarios are deterministic.'
scenarios: An executor completing 'malformed' handling cannot tell whether the AC is done unless the expected outcome (e.g., pin_status=malformed-pin, valid=false when enforced) is stated.
A reviewer cannot confirm the AC is met without knowing the expected output for each of the five sub-scenarios.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command per AC group (e.g., `pytest tests/attestation/test_pin_health.py -k 'drift and claim'`) so each acceptance criterion maps to a verifiable in-session check.
evidence: 'Run focused attestation invalidation, reuse, drift-refresh, re-sign, and config tests.' — no test path, selector, or command given
ABSENCE: no `pytest -k`, `make test-...`, or equivalent command anchored to any AC
findings.finding: The Verification section provides only a category description ('Run focused attestation invalidation, reuse, drift-refresh, re-sign, and config tests') rather than concrete proving commands. No AC has a named test file, test function, or invocation that would produce in-session evidence of passing.
findings.location: Verification section
scenarios: An executor cannot tell which test covers 'Enabled pin drift prevents re-sign' vs 'Audit reports enabled pin drift as invalid' — both map to the same undifferentiated 'Run focused...tests' sentence.
If the tests pass but don't cover a specific AC path, the completion verdict cannot be confirmed.
findings.suggested_fix: Group ACs by test module and add explicit invocations: e.g., `pytest tests/attestation/test_pin_invalidation.py` for ACs 1-6, `pytest tests/attestation/test_pin_health.py` for ACs 7-8, `pytest tests/attestation/test_pin_config.py` for ACs 9-14. Add `grep -c '"pin_status"' <event-log>` as an absence check for AC 13.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Align AC terminology: replace 'malformed manifest metadata' with the precise concept (e.g., 'a malformed pin entry (malformed-pin)' and/or 'an unparseable manifest') to match the pin_status vocabulary defined in the Implementation.
evidence: AC text: 'Malformed manifest metadata returns malformed/invalid at every consumer without raising.'
Implementation text: 'pin_status exactly current, stale-pin-drift, stale-pin-missing, malformed-pin, or legacy-unpinned'
Implementation text: 'When enabled, drift, malformed pins, or a deleted/missing pinned target invalidate the attestation.'
'Manifest metadata' is not defined elsewhere in the plan; 'malformed-pin' refers specifically to a bad pin entry, not a malformed manifest document.
findings.finding: The AC 'Malformed manifest metadata returns malformed/invalid at every consumer without raising' uses the term 'malformed manifest metadata', but the Implementation uses 'malformed-pin' as the pin_status value and 'malformed pins' as the concept — it is ambiguous whether the AC refers to a malformed pin entry, a malformed manifest document, or both.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: 'Malformed manifest metadata returns malformed/invalid at every consumer without raising'; Implementation: pin_status values including 'malformed-pin'
scenarios: Implementer may test only malformed pin entries, missing the case where the entire manifest metadata block is unparseable (or vice versa).
'Without raising' behavior is untested if the scope of 'malformed manifest metadata' is unclear.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'Malformed manifest metadata returns malformed/invalid at every consumer without raising' with two ACs: 'A malformed-pin entry returns pin_status=malformed-pin and invalid at every consumer without raising' and 'An unparseable plan manifest returns malformed/invalid without raising' (if the latter is in scope).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the decision rule for `DerivedPlanReviewHealth.enforced`: specify that it reflects the config value read once at health-compute call time (with the fail-open default of false when config is unreadable), co-located with the field definition.
evidence: Plan text: 'enforced boolean' listed as a field of DerivedPlanReviewHealth
Plan text: 'When enabled, drift ... invalidate the attestation. When disabled, report the same observations as advisory while valid remains true.'
ABSENCE: No rule states whether `enforced` is read from config at health-compute call time, from a snapshot of config at some earlier point, or derived from some other source.
ABSENCE: No rule states what happens when config is unreadable at health-compute time (the fail-open warning rule applies to the gate, but DerivedPlanReviewHealth.enforced would need a value).
findings.finding: The `DerivedPlanReviewHealth.enforced` boolean has no specified decision rule for when and how it is set. The plan states 'enforced boolean' as a field but never explains whether it reflects the config state at health-compute time, whether it is re-evaluated on every read, or whether it captures a snapshot at signing time. Under advisory mode with the config toggled mid-pipeline, the value is ambiguous.
findings.location: Implementation section — DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract definition
scenarios: A caller inspects `health.enforced` to display whether enforcement is active; if config is unreadable, should `enforced` be false (fail-open) or null/omitted?
Two renderers read `enforced` at different clock ticks with a concurrent config toggle — they get different values for the same attestation.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the DerivedPlanReviewHealth definition: '`enforced` reflects the value of `verify.enforce_plan_material_pins` read once at health-compute call time; defaults to false when config is unreadable (matching the gate's fail-open posture).'

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete absence-proving command for the 'no warning event persisted' AC, such as `jq -e '[.events[] | select(.type=="warning" and .source=="derived_health")] | length == 0' <fixture-output>`.
evidence: ABSENCE: no grep/jq/DB query command stated for the absence criterion
'No warning event is persisted' — proving a negative requires an explicit structured-artifact check
findings.finding: AC 'No warning event is persisted for derived health' requires an absence assertion — that no event record is written — but the plan provides no artifact query or log-inspection command that would confirm the absence in-session.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'No warning event is persisted for derived health.'
scenarios: An executor could implement the feature, run make check (which passes), and still have warning events being silently persisted — the absence is never verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that the 'close-profile consumer' (AC 2, Implementation close-profile paragraph) has a current done-definition (a written story with its own ACs) before designing and testing a close-profile validity variant; if the close gate story is future work, remove AC 2 and the close-profile profile description from this story's scope to avoid YAGNI premature abstraction.
evidence: Codebase search for 'require_plan_review_for_close', 'plan_review.*close.*gate', 'close.*plan.*review' in src/rebar returns zero hits

transition_close.py:1-30 module docstring references completion-verification and plan-review gate only in the 'plan-review gate and parent-first cascade have run' sense — but that is the upstream transition_compute caller, not a close-specific plan-review validity gate
_config_schema.py:571-589 _SECTIONS["verify"] has no 'require_plan_review_for_close' key
AC 2 names 'close-profile consumer when that gate is active' — the gate itself has no story, no AC for enabling it, and no config key defined in this plan
findings.finding: The 'close-profile consumer' in AC 2 ('Enabled pin drift invalidates the close-profile consumer when that gate is active') implies a plan-review gate on the close path that does not currently exist: transition_close.py has no plan-review gate, no require_plan_review_for_close key is in VerifyConfig/_SECTIONS, and no call to claim_gate_check appears in the close path. The plan specifies 'the close story must call this profile rather than reimplement validity' — which is a YAGNI forward-spec driving a new config gate, a new AC, and a new profile variant for a consumer whose story has not been written, making this premature abstraction.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'the close profile sets check_code_freshness=false … the close story must call this profile rather than reimplement validity'; AC 2 — 'Enabled pin drift invalidates the close-profile consumer when that gate is active'
scenarios: Implementor adds a new close-profile variant and config gate that has no done-definition in any current story, pulling in unspecified scope
The 'close story' the plan defers to is never written, leaving the close-profile abstraction permanently unused

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether this ticket introduces the plan-review close gate in `transition_close.py`/`gates.py` (add to file_impact) or defers it to a dependent story, and update AC line 2 to reflect which scope is actually covered.
evidence: No grep hit for plan_review in transition_close.py
transition_close.py:444 shows only completion-verifier gate; no plan-review gate at close
Plan says 'the close story must call this profile' — implying the close story is a dependent/future story, not an existing consumer
findings.finding: The 'close-profile consumer' (AC: 'Enabled pin drift invalidates the close-profile consumer when that gate is active') refers to a plan-review gate at close time. No such gate exists today — `transition_close.py` contains only the completion-verifier close gate and the open-children guard; there is no plan-review attestation check at close. This is new-behavior creation, yet the plan presents it as an existing consumer being enumerated.
findings.location: AC line 2: 'Enabled pin drift invalidates the close-profile consumer when that gate is active'; Implementation: 'the close story must call this profile rather than reimplement validity'
scenarios: A reviewer examining the plan may assume the close gate already exists and only needs hooking into the new validity function, when in reality introducing the close-time plan-review gate is itself out-of-scope creation for a dependent story.
If the plan intends this ticket to introduce the close gate, that creation work is unscoped.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'archived' explicitly — state the field/flag on the target record that marks it archived and clarify that archived targets produce pin_status=current (or a specific status) rather than stale-pin-missing.
evidence: 'deleted/missing pinned target invalidate the attestation' — one behavioral rule
'Archived targets remain readable' — a contradictory-seeming rule with no definition of 'archived'
ABSENCE: no field, flag, or lookup mechanism is described for distinguishing archived from deleted/missing
findings.finding: The plan distinguishes 'deleted/missing pinned target' (which invalidates) from 'archived targets remain readable' but does not define what makes a target 'archived' vs 'deleted/missing', leaving the executor to invent the distinction.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'When enabled, drift, malformed pins, or a deleted/missing pinned target invalidate the attestation. Archived targets remain readable.'
scenarios: An executor implementing the validity check for a target that no longer exists in the registry must decide: is this 'missing' (invalidate) or possibly 'archived' (readable/valid)? No signal is given.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add a sentence to the Implementation section describing how verify.enforce_plan_material_pins interacts with new pin emission (confirming they are independent), or note that pin emission is out of scope and move these ACs to the relevant plan.
evidence: AC text (both): 'verify.enforce_plan_material_pins=[true/false] leaves new pin emission enabled.'
Implementation text: 'Do not rewrite pins when configuration changes.' — this is the only pin-emission-adjacent statement and concerns existing pins, not new emission.
ABSENCE: No Implementation sentence describes pin emission logic, the system responsible for emitting pins, or how the enforcement flag interacts with it.
findings.finding: Two ACs assert pin emission behavior ('verify.enforce_plan_material_pins=true leaves new pin emission enabled' and '…=false leaves new pin emission enabled'), but the Implementation section contains no described work about pin emission — it only states 'Do not rewrite pins when configuration changes', which is a negative constraint on existing pins, not a positive description of new pin emission behavior.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: 'verify.enforce_plan_material_pins=true leaves new pin emission enabled' and 'verify.enforce_plan_material_pins=false leaves new pin emission enabled'
scenarios: Implementer has no described mechanism to verify; the ACs test a behavior of a system (pin emitter) not described in this plan.
If pin emission is handled by a separate subsystem, these ACs may belong to that subsystem's plan rather than this one.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out procedure for verify.enforce_plan_material_pins=true (e.g., 'set flag to false; no pin data is modified; previously-invalid attestations return to valid; no additional migration step required') so the enablement step is reversible by documented procedure.
evidence: Implementation text: 'once enforcement is successfully enabled, validation fails closed.'
Implementation text: 'Do not rewrite pins when configuration changes.' — implies toggling the flag back off is possible, but this is not stated as a rollback procedure.

No section describes: who performs rollback, what the back-out steps are, whether any state change accompanies enabling enforcement that must also be reversed, or what 'successfully enabled' means operationally.
T4 criterion: 'The remedy is an explicit rollback/back-out plan or expand-contract sequencing — acknowledgement alone is insufficient.'
findings.finding: The plan enables a flag (verify.enforce_plan_material_pins=true) that causes previously-valid attestations to become invalid ('fails closed'), but provides no explicit rollback/back-out plan for reverting an enforcement enablement that causes unintended breakage in production.
findings.location: Implementation: 'once enforcement is successfully enabled, validation fails closed'; 'Keep optional unreadable configuration fail-open with a warning'
scenarios: Operator enables enforcement; unexpected attestations become invalid and block workflows; there is no documented procedure to safely revert.
'Do not rewrite pins when configuration changes' ensures data integrity on toggle, but operational rollback steps (config change, re-validation sweep, communication) are absent.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Rollback' note to the Implementation: 'Disabling enforcement (setting verify.enforce_plan_material_pins back to false) is sufficient to restore the previous valid=true behavior for all affected attestations; no pin data or attestation records are modified by toggling the flag.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or AGENTS.md entry capturing the DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract shape, its extension point for child stories (phase_status), and the rationale for consolidating all consumers onto a single compute_plan_review_validity profile boundary.
evidence: No mention of ADR, AGENTS.md, or design-doc update anywhere in the plan
The contract shape and its deliberate extension point for child 5967 is a novel architectural decision that future maintainers need context for
'Own one compute_plan_review_validity profile boundary used by claim, reuse, drift refresh, re-sign, audit/health, and close' — a significant consolidation with no recorded rationale
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract with a cross-story extension point (child 5967 adds phase_status/compatibility) and consolidates six consumers onto one validity profile — a non-trivial architectural decision — but names no ADR, AGENTS.md entry, or design-doc update to capture it.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Own the nested DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract' and 'Child 5967 extends the same contract'
scenarios: A future engineer adds a new consumer and is unaware of the contract extension pattern, duplicating logic instead
Child 5967 author misinterprets the extension point without documented intent

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define at least one structured log event or metric (e.g., a counter per pin_status outcome and an enforcement-mode-active gauge) so operators can observe pin drift detections, enforcement invalidations, and config-unreadable warnings in production.
evidence: No mention of log events, metrics, or traces for pin drift, malformed-pin, or enforcement-failure outcomes
'No warning event is persisted for derived health' removes a natural observability hook
Without instrumentation, an operator cannot distinguish 'enforcement disabled' from 'enforcement enabled but no drift' from 'enforcement enabled and rejecting' in aggregate
findings.finding: No instrumentation (structured log events, metrics, or traces) is planned for the new failure points introduced by pin enforcement — specifically pin-drift detection, malformed-pin detection, enforcement-mode invalidations, and the config-unreadable warning path. The AC 'No warning event is persisted for derived health' explicitly removes one observability path without substituting another, leaving operators blind to why attestations are being rejected in production.
findings.location: AC: 'No warning event is persisted for derived health'; Implementation: 'Keep optional unreadable configuration fail-open with a warning'
scenarios: Enforcement is silently misconfigured (e.g., config unreadable, fail-open) and no one notices because there is no metric/alert
Pin drift invalidations spike in production with no observable signal until users report failures
findings.suggested_fix: Emit a structured log line (warn level) for each enforcement invalidation with fields: attestation_id, pin_status, enforced=true/false; expose a counter metric attestation_pin_check_total{status, enforced}. This satisfies the 'no warning event persisted' AC (log ≠ persisted event) while providing observability.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a motivation / reproduction section that names a concrete current-behavior defect or user-visible gap (e.g., 'consumer X currently accepts a drifted pin because it lacks the check; this causes Y') to justify the new enforcement mechanism before building it.
evidence: Goal states: 'Use signed pins as the single related-material validity check across every attestation lifecycle consumer' — aspirational framing, not diagnostic.
No section describes a failing scenario, a bug, a regression, or a gap in current behavior that this change corrects.
No Expected/Actual pair or repro steps are present anywhere in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan proposes adding `verify.enforce_plan_material_pins`, a new `DerivedPlanReviewHealth` contract, and a unified `compute_plan_review_validity` profile boundary, but never demonstrates that the current behavior is wrong or insufficient — no reproduction, no Expected/Actual, no named defect, and no concrete user/system problem the current scattered validity checks cause.
findings.location: Goal / Implementation sections
scenarios: An agent building this plan has no baseline to validate correctness against — it cannot distinguish a correct implementation from one that already matches current behavior, because 'current behavior is wrong' is never established.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a short 'Current behavior / gap' subsection under Goal that names at least one concrete scenario where the current lack of unified pin enforcement causes observable wrong behavior (e.g., a stale-pin drift going undetected at reuse), and reference it from the relevant ACs.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` (new `enforce_plan_material_pins: bool = False` field on `VerifyConfig`) and `rebar.toml` to the edit targets for `verify.enforce_plan_material_pins`.
evidence: VerifyConfig dataclass is at src/rebar/_config_schema.py:211 — all verify.* flags are declared here
rebar.toml has [verify] section at line 5 documenting all verify.* flags
No mention of _config_schema.py in the plan's implementation or file_impact
findings.finding: The `verify.enforce_plan_material_pins` config key requires a new field on `VerifyConfig` in `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` and documentation in `rebar.toml`, but neither file is listed in the plan's edit targets (the plan only mentions `attest.py`, `manifest.py`, `orchestrator.py`, `workflow_ops.py`, and test files).
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Add verify.enforce_plan_material_pins with default false'
scenarios: The new config key will not be recognized by the config loader or typed schema without a new field on VerifyConfig.

The `gate_enabled` helper in gates.py reads `getattr(load_config(cfg_root).verify, attr)` — a missing field silently returns the default via fail-open, which may mask a typo rather than being intentional.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `enforce_plan_material_pins: bool = False` to the `VerifyConfig` dataclass in `_config_schema.py`, and add a commented entry to `rebar.toml [verify]` section matching the pattern of other opt-in gates.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief positive rationale for why `DerivedPlanReviewHealth` is a separate nested object (e.g., 'to allow child 5967 to extend it with phase_status without changing the top-level {valid, reason, verdict} contract that all consumers already key on').
evidence: Plan: 'Own the nested DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract returned as health beside compute_plan_review_validity existing top-level {valid, reason, verdict}.'
No sentence in the plan explains why a separate nested object is preferable to enriching the existing dict.
Child 5967 extends 'the same contract with phase_status and phase compatibility' — the extension rationale is present but the original split is not justified.
findings.finding: The plan provides no positive rationale for why `DerivedPlanReviewHealth` is a SEPARATE returned object beside the existing `{valid, reason, verdict}` top-level result, rather than enriching the existing result dict with a `health` subkey or adding `pin_status`/`targets` directly to the existing verdict. The absence of this rationale leaves the interface-boundary choice unjustified, and an implementer might reasonably fold it into the existing dict (saving a new type) or over-engineer a full class hierarchy — either of which could misalign with child 5967's extension.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Own the nested DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract returned as health beside compute_plan_review_validity existing top-level {valid, reason, verdict}'
scenarios: An implementer folds DerivedPlanReviewHealth into the existing {valid, reason, verdict} dict to avoid a new type; child 5967 then needs to add phase_status to a flat dict rather than the structured health object — coupling that was meant to be avoided.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence: 'The health object is nested rather than inlined into the top-level result so that child 5967 can add phase_status and compatibility fields without changing the {valid, reason, verdict} contract all six consumers already key on, and so the health object can be returned independently by the audit/health surface without forcing a full validity re-run.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Compare `DerivedPlanReviewHealth` against the existing `{valid, reason, verdict}` return contract and justify the nested struct pattern over enriching the flat dict with additional optional keys (e.g., `pin_status`, `enforced`, `targets`).
evidence: No codebase match for 'DerivedPlanReviewHealth'
Existing compute_validity returns a flat dict {valid, reason, verdict} — all consumers key on these three fields
audit/page.py builds its own _plan_round view dict from the payload; no nested health sub-struct precedent exists
findings.finding: The plan introduces `DerivedPlanReviewHealth` as a novel nested contract returned *beside* (not replacing) the existing `{valid, reason, verdict}` dict — a new pattern in this codebase. No prior-art comparison is made to the existing `compute_validity` return shape, the audit `_plan_round` view dict, or any established health-reporting contract. The plan does not justify why a nested struct (rather than enriching the existing return dict with additional keys) is the right architecture, violating the anti-repackaging check.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Own the nested DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract returned as health beside compute_plan_review_validity existing top-level {valid, reason, verdict}.'
scenarios: Renderers that currently consume {valid, reason, verdict} must be updated to handle the new nested health key — the plan says 'all renderers consume this contract rather than duplicating strings' but does not enumerate which renderers or how the migration is gated
The nested struct pattern creates an implicit versioning problem when child 5967 adds phase_status: callers that ignored the health sub-struct silently miss phase incompatibility

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the `ManifestFormatError` dependency: either define it in this story's manifest module and confirm it aligns with the parent plan's 'without raising' AC, or explicitly state that malformed detection returns a sentinel (consistent with existing parsers) and update child 5967's language accordingly.
evidence: No codebase search match for 'ManifestFormatError'
AC line: 'Malformed manifest metadata returns malformed/invalid at every consumer without raising.' — contradicts 'raises ManifestFormatError' in the child story description
Existing manifest parsers (manifest_regver, manifest_deps, manifest_material) return None/empty on malformed input rather than raising
findings.finding: The plan references `ManifestFormatError` as the exception raised by the new `manifest_review_phase()` and `manifest_priority_floor()` parsers (child 5967's contract), but `ManifestFormatError` does not exist anywhere in the codebase. This is an unverified capability assertion: the plan implicitly requires this exception class to exist before child 5967 can be implemented. The current plan (the parent story) does not define it, yet the pin-parsing (`malformed-pin`) and phase-parsing share the same 'raises on malformed' contract — if the parent story introduces malformed-pin detection it must define the exception class first.
findings.location: Child 5967 description: 'Either raises ManifestFormatError for duplicates, missing/extra tokens, unknown phases, nonnumeric values, or values outside [0.0,1.0].' Cross-referenced with AC: 'Malformed manifest metadata returns malformed/invalid at every consumer without raising.'
scenarios: Implementer of the parent story introduces malformed-pin detection without defining ManifestFormatError, leaving child 5967 blocked
The 'without raising' AC in the parent plan contradicts the 'raises ManifestFormatError' language in child 5967, causing implementers to make incompatible choices
findings.suggested_fix: Adopt the existing parser convention: return a sentinel value (e.g., `None` or a typed `MalformedPin` dataclass) for malformed pin lines rather than raising, keeping the 'without raising' AC. Child 5967 can raise `ManifestFormatError` for its distinct parse-time validation (duplicates, unknown phases) as a separate concern.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Compare the dual-mode advisory/enforcing validity pattern against the existing binary gate-enable precedents (`require_plan_review_for_claim`, etc.) and justify why a dual-mode validity reporter is preferable over a simpler 'skip pin check entirely when disabled, run it fail-closed when enabled' approach.
evidence: All existing VerifyConfig flags (require_plan_review_for_claim, require_completion_verification_for_close, overlap_enabled) act as binary gate-enables, not dual-mode validity reporters
compute_validity currently returns {valid, reason, verdict} with no advisory/enforcing distinction
No codebase match for any dual-mode validity pattern
findings.finding: The plan introduces a dual-mode advisory/enforcing validity pattern (enabled = fail-closed on drift; disabled = advisory only, valid remains true) with no comparison to how similar enforcement toggles are implemented elsewhere in the codebase. The existing enforcement toggles (`require_plan_review_for_claim`, `require_completion_verification_for_close`, `opcert_enforce_since`) all operate as binary gate enables — not dual-mode validity reporters. The plan does not justify why a dual-mode validity function (rather than a separate advisory reporting step alongside a binary gate check) is preferable, nor cite any prior art for this pattern in the project.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'When enabled, drift, malformed pins, or a deleted/missing pinned target invalidate the attestation. When disabled, report the same observations as advisory while valid remains true.'
scenarios: Future implementers conflate the advisory vs enforcing modes in the six consumers, accidentally treating advisory observations as blocking

The 'valid remains true' advisory mode creates a new semantic: a validity check that returns valid=True while also reporting drift — callers must handle a new 'valid but degraded' response shape they have never seen before

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assert the post-rollback schema state: explicitly document (and test) that a rolled-back manifest reader silently ignores unknown `plan-material-pin:` lines in already-persisted SIGNATURE events, so existing attestations remain readable and valid after a code rollback.
evidence: Plan body: 'TargetPinDetail is exactly {canonical_id: string, role: child|prerequisite, pinned_fingerprint: string, current_fingerprint: string|null, pin_status: current|stale-pin-drift|stale-pin-missing|malformed-pin}'
Plan body never names a rollback state assertion for the persisted manifest format
Existing manifest.py parsers scan by prefix (e.g. `s.startswith(_DEP_PREFIX + ' ')`) — implying unknown lines are ignored — but the plan does not make this a stated guarantee
ABSENCE: no plan section describes 'what rolled-back code does when it reads a pin-bearing SIGNATURE event already in the event log'
Cached prior review finding: 'The plan introduces `plan-material-pin:` lines into cryptographically-signed persisted manifests but never asserts the schema/manifest state AFTER a code rollback'
findings.finding: The plan introduces new `plan-material-pin:` lines into cryptographically-signed, immutably-persisted SIGNATURE events, but never asserts the manifest/schema STATE AFTER a code rollback. If a rollback occurs after pin-bearing attestations are appended to the event log, the rolled-back code encounters unknown manifest lines; the plan never declares whether rolled-back verifiers/readers ignore those lines (safe) or fail closed (DATA LOSS RISK: a valid attestation becomes unreadable). The existing manifest parsers appear to be prefix-scan, but this backward-compat contract is not asserted by the plan.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Compare signed pins against current narrow material to populate it'; AC 'Malformed manifest metadata returns malformed/invalid at every consumer without raising'
scenarios: Feature ships; several tickets accumulate pin-bearing SIGNATURE events; a regression forces a code rollback; rolled-back verifiers must decide whether pin-bearing attestations are valid, invalid, or malformed — the plan does not specify the answer.

Enforcement is enabled, pins are written, rollback happens, old code reads the attestation — if old code treats the unknown lines as 'malformed manifest' the attestation becomes invalid, blocking claim/close gates even though no material drift occurred (false invalidation).
findings.suggested_fix: Add a stated backward-compat contract: 'Any manifest line whose prefix is not recognized by the current code is silently skipped; this applies specifically to `plan-material-pin:` lines encountered by pre-feature code after rollback.' Add a regression test that feeds a pin-bearing manifest to the old `build_manifest` / `manifest_material` path and asserts it does not raise and returns valid.

findings.finding: The plan's close-profile consumer is under-specified: the current close path in transition_close.py does not invoke any plan-review validity check (it calls verify_completion, the LLM completion-verifier), so 'the close story must call this profile rather than reimplement validity' requires NEW wiring whose insertion point, config condition, and interaction with the existing completion-verifier gate are not described. Without this, the 'close profile' cannot be reliably implemented as a reuse rather than a duplication.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'the close profile sets check_code_freshness=false while retaining signature, registry, phase, own-material, and configured pin checks; the close story must call this profile rather than reimplement validity'

findings.finding: manifest_review_phase() and manifest_priority_floor() are named in AC 15 as functions the completion-verifier branch must NOT invoke, but neither their manifest-line format (prefix string, field layout), vocabulary, nor return values are defined anywhere in this plan. An executor must invent this spec.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC 15: 'The completion-verifier branch never invokes manifest_review_phase(), manifest_priority_floor(), or manifest_pins()'

findings.finding: The plan defines phase_status (compatible/incompatible/malformed) in DerivedPlanReviewHealth but never specifies the decision rule: what observable fact(s) about an attestation's manifest data cause phase_status to be 'incompatible' vs 'compatible' vs 'malformed'. Without this rule, ACs 16–20 (phase-incompatible attestations fail claim, close, reuse, re-sign, audit) are unimplementable.
findings.location: Implementation section — DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract; Acceptance Criteria AC 16–20

findings.finding: ACs 16–20 require phase-incompatibility enforcement across all consumers (claim, close, reuse, re-sign, audit, derived health), but the Implementation body only names the phase_status field in the health contract without describing what constitutes a phase mismatch, how phase_status is populated, or what logic drives phase-incompatibility detection. There is no described deliverable implementing this behavior.
findings.location: Implementation (DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract description) vs. AC lines: 'Phase-incompatible attestations fail claim validity', 'Phase-incompatible attestations fail close-profile validity', 'Phase-incompatible attestations cannot be reused', 'Phase-incompatible attestations cannot be re-signed', 'Audit plus derived health report phase incompatibility'
findings.suggested_fix: Under the DerivedPlanReviewHealth description, add: 'phase_status is set by comparing the attestation's recorded phase against the current manifest phase at validation time; incompatible when they differ; malformed when the manifest phase field is unreadable/missing.'

findings.finding: The AC 'The completion-verifier branch never invokes manifest_review_phase(), manifest_priority_floor(), or manifest_pins(); completion attestations carrying those unexpected lines do not raise ManifestFormatError' references a 'completion-verifier branch' and three named functions that appear nowhere in the Implementation body. There is no described deliverable corresponding to this criterion.
findings.location: AC: 'The completion-verifier branch never invokes manifest_review_phase(), manifest_priority_floor(), or manifest_pins()...'

findings.finding: The Verification section provides only two generic 'run tests' bullets ('Run focused attestation invalidation, reuse, drift-refresh, re-sign, and config tests' and 'Run make check and the non-integration test suite') with no specific test names, file paths, or command invocations. None of the 20 acceptance criteria has a concrete proving command that would produce evidence on success, violating E6(a) and E6(c).
findings.location: Verification section; all 20 Acceptance Criteria lines

findings.finding: The plan introduces `plan-material-pin:` lines into cryptographically-signed persisted manifests but never asserts the schema/manifest state AFTER a code rollback. If pre-feature code is redeployed after pin-bearing manifests exist: (1) old verifier code may reject manifests with unknown pin lines, rendering all pin-bearing attestations unverifiable and blocking every claim gate; (2) old resign_plan_review / refresh_attestation will silently emit a pinless manifest when re-signing, dropping pin provenance without error — DATA LOSS RISK. The plan's pre-feature-verifier fixture only covers NEW code reading OLD manifests, not the reverse.
findings.location: Implementation section ('Compare signed pins against current narrow material…') and AC line 'Missing/deleted, archived, malformed, duplicate, and re-enable scenarios are deterministic'; also 'Do not rewrite pins when configuration changes.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion verifying that all renderers consume DerivedPlanReviewHealth rather than duplicating health strings (e.g., no renderer constructs pin_status strings independently).
evidence: Implementation text: '…all renderers consume this contract rather than duplicating strings.'
Scanning all 14 ACs: none mention renderers, string deduplication, or the renderer refactor.
ABSENCE: No AC verifies renderer adoption of DerivedPlanReviewHealth.
findings.finding: The Implementation describes 'all renderers consume this contract rather than duplicating strings' as a deliverable, but no acceptance criterion covers this renderer migration.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'all renderers consume this contract rather than duplicating strings'
scenarios: Renderers could continue duplicating health strings and all ACs would still pass, leaving the described deliverable unverified.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
